failure_surface: Code execution succeeded but evaluation returned failure (test_case_results=[0]). Agent placed 'WIN RATE' text in adjacent cell C8 instead of appending it to the percentage display in cell B8 as instructed.
root_cause: Misinterpretation of the instruction 'append the text WIN RATE to the displayed percentage result'. Agent implemented text in adjacent cell rather than modifying cell B8's content/format to include both percentage and text in the same cell.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: When instructions specify appending text to a displayed value in the same cell, apply Excel custom number formatting to the numeric value rather than placing text in a separate adjacent cell.
evidence: Instruction states: 'append the text WIN RATE to the displayed percentage result without altering the number format which should show as an integer percentage (e.g., 67% WIN RATE)' - example clearly shows both in same cell
Agent placed text in ws.cell(row=8, column=3).value = 'WIN RATE' (cell C8) instead of modifying B8
Multiple execution attempts resulted in SyntaxError due to malformed code block markers (<python>, <code> prefixes), suggesting execution hygiene issues
Final successful execution still violated the cell-location requirement regardless of execution success

memory_items.title: Interpret Text Appending Instructions Literally
memory_items.lesson: When instructions specify appending text to a displayed value in a target cell, modify the target cell's format or value—not place the text in adjacent cells.
memory_items.generalization: Cell-level manipulation tasks requiring text to be appended to calculated displays should use Excel custom number formats or concatenation formulas in the same cell.

memory_items.title: Preserve Calculation Capability When Adding Display Text
memory_items.lesson: If a cell must display both a numeric value and text while remaining functional for calculations, use Excel's custom number format feature rather than converting to plain text.
memory_items.generalization: Custom number formats (e.g., '0.00% WIN RATE') allow numeric cells to display supplemental text without losing their ability to participate in further computations.

memory_items.title: Use Standard Code Block Delimiters for Execution
memory_items.lesson: Prefer triple-backtick fenced code blocks (```python ... ```) over alternative markdown markers to ensure execution environments parse Python code correctly.
memory_items.generalization: Non-standard code block syntax (prefixing with <python> or <code>) causes repeated syntax errors; always verify code executes cleanly before relying on the implementation.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Text Appending in Target Cells
When instructions specify appending text to a displayed value in a target cell, do not place the text in an adjacent cell. Use Excel custom number formats to display supplementary text alongside numeric values within the same cell.

### Custom Number Format for Combined Display
To show both a calculated percentage and descriptive text (e.g., '67% WIN RATE'), set the cell's number_format property to a pattern like '0.00%" WIN RATE"' rather than writing concatenated text. This preserves the cell's numeric type for any downstream calculations.

### Execution Hygiene: Code Block Syntax
Always wrap executable Python code in standard triple-backtick fences (\`\`\`python). Avoid alternative delimiters or prefix markers that may cause execution environment parsing errors.
